title: Dog Prostate Enlargement: Symptoms, Causes, and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn the symptoms of dog prostate enlargement, when it becomes an emergency, and how capturing photos can assist your vet in diagnosis.
content_html:
Understanding Prostate Enlargement in Dogs

Prostate enlargement, often medically referred to as Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH), is a common condition in older, intact male dogs. It occurs when the prostate gland increases in size due to hormonal influences, which can eventually put pressure on the urethra and the rectum, causing discomfort and bathroom issues.

Urgency Level: Medium

Prostate enlargement is generally classified as a medium urgency level. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a progressive condition that requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog is completely unable to pass urine, this is a critical emergency and you should seek immediate care at an ER facility.

Symptoms to Monitor

Typical symptoms include straining to defecate, producing flat or ribbon-shaped stools, and blood-tinged fluid dripping from the penis independently of urination. Some dogs may also exhibit a stiff-legged gait or persistent urinary tract infections.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When consulting with a vet or triage assistant, providing photos or videos can be vital. A photo of any abnormal discharge, the shape of the stool, or a video of your dog's posture while attempting to urinate helps the clinical team assess the level of straining and discomfort, allowing them to better prioritize your dog's care.

faqs.question: Can neutered dogs get prostate enlargement?
faqs.answer: While Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia is almost exclusive to intact dogs, neutered dogs can still develop prostate issues such as prostatitis (infection) or prostatic carcinoma (cancer).

faqs.question: What is the primary treatment for an enlarged prostate?
faqs.answer: For most cases of hormonal enlargement, neutering is the recommended treatment as it naturally causes the prostate gland to shrink.

faqs.question: Is blood in the urine always a sign of prostate issues?
faqs.answer: No, blood in the urine can also indicate bladder stones, urinary tract infections, or kidney issues, which is why a professional diagnosis is necessary.
